claim: Agentic workflows (iterative agent loops with reflection and revision) dramatically outperform zero-shot prompting on coding tasks, with GPT-3.5 in agentic mode beating GPT-4 zero-shot
actionable: Implement agentic loops that have LLMs write code, test it, read results, think about failures, and revise iteratively rather than asking for code in a single prompt
source_title: What's next for AI agentic workflows ft. Andrew Ng of AI Fund

claim: Self-reflection (prompting LLM to check its own code for correctness and fix bugs) is a robust and reliable design pattern that works consistently for most applications
actionable: After generating code, prompt the LLM with: 'Here is code intended for task X. Check the code carefully for correctness, sound efficiency, good construction, critical issues. If there are problems, fix them.'
source_title: What's next for AI agentic workflows ft. Andrew Ng of AI Fund

claim: Running unit tests and having LLMs fix failures based on test output is a practical way to improve code quality and guide iterative refinement
actionable: Have LLM run unit tests, collect failure messages, feed them back asking 'Why did the test fail? What should you change to fix it?' and iterate until tests pass
source_title: What's next for AI agentic workflows ft. Andrew Ng of AI Fund

claim: Multi-agent collaboration where one LLM acts as coder and another as code reviewer improves code quality more than single-agent approaches, and can use the same base model prompted differently
actionable: Implement two-agent systems where Agent 1 (prompt: 'You are an expert coder') writes code, and Agent 2 (prompt: 'You are an expert code reviewer') reviews it, then iterate
source_title: What's next for AI agentic workflows ft. Andrew Ng of AI Fund

claim: Tool use (agents calling APIs and external functions) significantly expands what LLMs can accomplish by enabling web search, code execution, image manipulation, and data retrieval
actionable: Design agents with access to function-calling capabilities for: web search APIs, code execution, image generation/detection, database queries, and specialized domain tools
source_title: What's next for AI agentic workflows ft. Andrew Ng of AI Fund

claim: Planning algorithms and task decomposition enable agents to handle multi-step complex tasks like image synthesis with multiple vision models and text-to-speech conversion
actionable: Have agents break complex multi-modal tasks into steps (extract pose → find model → synthesize image → extract text → synthesize speech) and chain model calls appropriately
source_title: What's next for AI agentic workflows ft. Andrew Ng of AI Fund

claim: Multi-agent debate where different LLMs discuss and argue about answers results in better performance than single-agent outputs
actionable: Set up debate systems where multiple agents (could be same model or different) argue about answers before reaching consensus, improving reasoning quality
source_title: What's next for AI agentic workflows ft. Andrew Ng of AI Fund

claim: Users need to learn to delegate tasks to AI agents and wait minutes or even hours for results rather than expecting instant responses, similar to delegating to humans
actionable: When using agents for complex tasks, accept that multi-step reasoning and iterative improvement takes time; set agents to work and check back later rather than blocking
source_title: What's next for AI agentic workflows ft. Andrew Ng of AI Fund

claim: Fast token generation speed matters more for agentic workflows than raw model quality, since agents iterate many times and fast inference enables more iterations in same time
actionable: When choosing models for agents, prefer faster token generation over marginal quality improvements, as iteration speed compounds in agentic loops
source_title: What's next for AI agentic workflows ft. Andrew Ng of AI Fund
